FT_IC1.evidence: US-based Adolescent Brain Cognitive Development (ABCD) Study with 1,415 youth participants from Year 4 follow-up (age ~14)

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying smartphone variables: passive app use (EARS), texting apps, social media apps. Paper reports separate app category use (social media and texting apps comprise ~50% of use). Applies B1 (separate per-category measurements).

FT_IC3.evidence: Correlation tests reported: r=0.33 (keystroke vs app use), rs=0.21–0.35 (passive measures vs self-report use)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric extractable: correlations (r=0.33, rs=0.21–0.35), means (5 h/day smartphone use), SDs, descriptive statistics in tables

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD data; validation and measurement characterization of passive smartphone sensing measures

FT_IC6.evidence: Novel 2024 publication; no evidence of preprint/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper is measurement/validation focused (not demographics→SM as DV in all analyses). Reports correlations and descriptive patterns by sex, but is not structured as longitudinal demographics-only predictors of SM.
notes: Measurement validation paper using objective passive sensing. Cross-sectional descriptive/validation design with ABCD EARS data.

FT_IC1.evidence: ABCD Study resource; focuses on the dataset involving 11,000+ US youth

FT_IC2.evidence: Resource/documentation paper does not report data on specific smartphone or SM variables

FT_IC3.evidence: No empirical statistical analyses; this is a documentation/resource paper

FT_IC4.evidence: No numeric data extracted from empirical analysis

FT_IC5.evidence: Resource/documentation paper (B14). Describes a help platform and discussion board for ABCD researchers; no original empirical analysis

FT_EC7_reason: FT_EC7 does not apply; FT_IC5 is NOT_MET (resource paper, not empirical analysis)
notes: Excluded at FT_IC5. Pure resource/documentation paper describing NowIKnowMyABCD platform.

FT_IC1.evidence: US ABCD Study with 11,868 participants followed from age ~9–10 to ~13–14

FT_IC2.evidence: Qualifying social media and related variables: social media, video chat, texting (measured as hours per day). Paper reports separate odds ratios for each technology subtype. Applies B1 (separate per-item coefficients for social media, video chat, texting).

FT_IC3.evidence: Multilevel logistic regression with ORs and 95% CIs for technology subtypes; e.g., females 234% greater odds of >2 hr/day social media (95% CI: 3.14–3.55)

FT_IC4.evidence: Numeric extractable: ORs with 95% CIs throughout (e.g., YouTube OR 1.25 [95% CI 1.16–1.35]; social media OR 3.34 [95% CI 3.14–3.55]); descriptive statistics

FT_IC5.evidence: Original empirical analysis using ABCD data; longitudinal and cross-sectional design examining technology use trajectories and sociodemographic associations

FT_IC6.evidence: Novel 2024 publication; no evidence of preprint/duplicate

FT_EC7_reason: Paper reports both cross-sectional and longitudinal analyses (B15 carve-out). Abstract states 'examined cross-sectional and longitudinal use patterns' and reports associations between technology subtypes and sociodemographics. Any cross-sectional analysis with demographics→SM disqualifies FT_EC7.
notes: Included; cross-sectional carve-out applies. Longitudinal technology use patterns with sociodemographic predictors (sex, parental education, income, marital status) in mixed designs.
